Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd spend maybe 24 hours a week on coursework, but it wasn't like I had a set schedule or anything. I'd often find myself listening to lecture recordings while doing other things around my apartment, since I learn better by hearing things anyway. Some weeks I'd be really into it, other weeks I'd barely touch anything until I absolutely had to.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what else was going on. I didn't really have a consistent weekly schedule - it was more like I'd study when the mood struck me or when something was due soon. Deadlines were usually what got me moving, but even then I wasn't always great about getting things submitted. I'm not really a planner, I just kind of go with the flow.
Q3: Usually I logged in because I got a notification about something or I was curious about what was happening in the discussion forums. Sometimes I'd just be browsing on my laptop and think 'oh, I should probably check the course.' I logged in pretty frequently actually, like 15 times or so, but that doesn't mean I was always doing productive work when I was there.
Q4: Yeah, checking the LMS kind of became a habit after a while, almost like checking social media. I'd open it up, scroll through some discussions, watch part of a video, interact with some classmates. The peer interaction part was actually kind of nice - I talked with people quite a bit on there. It felt more social than academic sometimes, which made it easier to keep coming back.
Q5: The video lectures were definitely the most useful for me since I'm an auditory learner. I watched over four hours of video content because I could really absorb information that way. The discussion forums were also helpful because hearing other people's perspectives and questions made concepts click better. When someone explained something in their own words, it often made more sense than the textbook.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the readings and any text-heavy resources. They just didn't stick with me the way audio content did. I also tended to skip the practice assignments unless they were graded - my assignment completion rate was pretty low, I know. If something felt like busywork or didn't seem immediately relevant, I'd put it off indefinitely.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was engaging and I could see how it connected to understanding the bigger picture. I liked things that sparked some kind of reaction in me - surprise or curiosity. If I felt like I was just going through the motions or checking a box, I'd lose interest fast. The mindfulness exercises we did actually surprised me by being helpful.
Q8: Optional activities felt like suggestions I could take or leave, while required ones had this weight to them even if I still didn't always complete them. With optional stuff, I'd only do it if I was genuinely interested or bored. Required activities came with guilt when I skipped them, but that guilt wasn't always enough to make me actually do them.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of audio files and lecture recordings to listen to when I wasn't near my computer. I'd put them on while walking around or doing chores. I also talked through concepts with friends outside of class, which helped me process things. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the screen.
Q10: There were tons of times I was learning but the LMS wouldn't know about it. Like when I'd replay a downloaded lecture three times while cooking dinner, or when I'd have long conversations with classmates over text about course concepts. I also did a lot of thinking and processing while doing mindfulness exercises, which obviously doesn't show up anywhere.
Q11: Yeah, all the time. I'd download a video or audio file once and then listen to it multiple times offline. The LMS would just show one access, but I might have engaged with that content five or six times. It was more convenient to have things saved locally so I didn't need internet or to be logged in.
Q12: There were definitely periods where I just couldn't bring myself to care about the course. Sometimes it was because I was distracted by other things in life, sometimes the material just felt dry. My attendance dropped quite a bit - I was only there about 63% of the time. When I wasn't feeling it, I really wasn't feeling it.
Q13: Competing responsibilities were the biggest thing. I have the resources to succeed, but that doesn't mean I always used them wisely. When I had other stuff going on, the LMS was the first thing to get neglected. Fatigue played a role too - sometimes I'd log in but just not have the mental energy to actually do anything meaningful.
Q14: Oh definitely. I'd log in, click through some materials, maybe have a video playing in the background while I was really thinking about something else entirely. My mind would wander and I'd realize I'd been staring at the same page for ten minutes without absorbing anything. Being present physically or digitally isn't the same as being mentally engaged.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, which is why my completion rate was so low. I knew they were important but there was always this disconnect between knowing and doing. Sometimes the task felt overwhelming, sometimes I just didn't feel like it. I'd tell myself I'd do it later and then later never came.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that could reliably get me to act. Without a deadline, I'd drift indefinitely. Even with deadlines, I'd often wait until the last possible moment. My exam score was decent because there was a clear deadline and consequence, but ongoing assignments without immediate pressure? Those slipped through the cracks.
Q17: Feedback did affect me when I actually received it. Getting comments on discussion posts made me want to participate more. But since I didn't submit many assignments, I didn't get much feedback to respond to. It's kind of a cycle - less submission means less feedback means less motivation to submit.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which was both good and bad for someone like me. Good because I could engage in ways that suited my learning style, bad because I needed more external structure to stay on track. The 15 online courses I was juggling made it hard to give any single one the attention it probably deserved.
Q19: Having video and audio content made engagement easier since that's how I learn best. The discussion forums were accessible and low-pressure. What made it harder was when things were text-heavy or when assignments required a lot of written output. Also, the sheer volume of content across all my courses was overwhelming sometimes.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty well actually. I was logged in a lot, watched videos, participated in discussions, but my actual assignment submissions were really low. For me, it was about engagement feeling different from production. I could be genuinely interested and learning without translating that into submitted work. The motivation to consume content is different from the motivation to create output.
Q21: That makes sense to me too. Some people are just efficient - they get what they need quickly and don't need to spend hours in the system. Or they're studying offline like I do with downloaded materials. The LMS activity doesn't capture everything. Someone could ace the exam by studying their own way without ever looking like a star student online.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns - when you're active, what you click on, how long you're there. But they miss the quality of attention, offline engagement, and whether someone is actually thinking deeply or just scrolling mindlessly. They can't capture conversations with friends about course material or that moment in the shower when a concept finally clicks.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning, being curious, wanting to understand rather than just get through it. It's about mental presence, not just physical or digital presence. For me, engagement looks like listening to a lecture and genuinely thinking about it, not just having it play in the background. It's internal, not external.
Q24: The LMS would never show how the mindfulness intervention actually helped my mood and approach to the course. It wouldn't capture the surprise I felt when certain concepts clicked, or the conversations I had with peers outside the platform. It also wouldn't show that despite my messy engagement patterns, I actually passed and learned things that mattered to me. The data tells a story, but it's not the whole story.
